one_line_takeaway: A benchmark (SafeEditBench, minimally-edited safe/unsafe pairs judged under 5 policies) plus a two-stage recaption-SFT + verifiable-reward-RL method (SafeGuard-VL on Qwen2.5-VL-7B) showing that existing image guardrails are policy-overfit, and that RL-based policy-conditioning improves cross-policy generalization without wrecking general VLM ability.
key_finding: Existing guardrails are policy-overfit, not policy-adaptive. Table 3 shows an SFT model trained on policy L1 degenerates into an 'always-safe' classifier (100 on L1, 0 on L2-L5); RL reduces but does not eliminate this. QwenGuard-7B scores 84.57 on its own LlavaGuardBench but collapses on UnsafeBench (43.56) and general tasks (General Overall 35.98, BLINK 12.05), i.e., over-specialization destroys generalization. SafeGuard-VL, trained on the same data, stays balanced: Ours(RL) gets 71.78 on LlavaGuardBench while lifting UnsafeBench 41.71->62.39 and keeping General Overall at 57.02. Ours(Full) reaches the best UnsafeBench overall of 72.2 (vs Qwen2.5-VL-7B 41.7, QwenGuard-7B 43.6, ShieldGemma2 47.3). Ablation confirms both stages matter: removing recaption drops UnsafeBench 66.96->53.22, and adding RL after SFT adds +5.2 (66.96->72.16), with general capability held stable (~53-57).
core_contribution: The paper makes two distinct contributions of its own (as opposed to the cited LlavaGuard/QwenGuard/UnsafeBench prior work it builds on and compares against). (1) SafeEditBench, a cross-policy image-guardrail benchmark that stresses whether a moderation model can re-judge the SAME image differently under FIVE heterogeneous safety policies (L1 most permissive to L5 most restrictive), rather than under a single fixed taxonomy. Its distinguishing design is semantically-aligned safe/unsafe image pairs: unsafe images from the LlavaGuard test set are minimally edited (via Gemini's 'Nano Banana' image editor) so the safe counterpart differs only in the localized policy-violating region (e.g., a weapon turned into a camera), forcing models to reason about the specific unsafe element rather than coarse scene cues. (2) SafeGuard-VL, a two-stage training method built on Qwen2.5-VL-7B: Stage-1 SFT teaches unsafe SEMANTICS via a 'self-recaption' pipeline (Qwen2.5-VL writes a sanitized caption, then the more permissive Gemma-27B minimally edits it to re-inject suppressed harmful details) instead of binary safe/unsafe classification labels; Stage-2 applies rule-based RL with verifiable rewards (GRPO), using the human safe/unsafe label as the reward, to teach policy-conditioned discrimination. The claimed novelty is decoupling semantic understanding (SFT) from policy-specific recognition (RL) so the guardrail generalizes to unseen/evolving policies while retaining general VLM ability.
threat_model: Defensive content-moderation setting. The adversary/failure surface is twofold: (i) harmful visual content (sexual, violence, self-harm, illegal, hate, shocking, deception, political, spam) that a deployed VLM must reject; and (ii) evasion via minimal edits/perturbations that keep the scene near-identical while altering only the policy-violating region, which the paper argues real malicious users exploit to bypass filters. A second, more novel 'threat' the paper foregrounds is policy drift: because 'safe' is defined by organization/jurisdiction/culture-specific policies that evolve over time, a guardrail overfit to one fixed policy silently fails when the policy changes. The paper is explicitly a guardrail/defense paper, not an attack paper.
limitations: SafeEditBench is small (128 images / 62 pairs) and derived entirely from the LlavaGuard test set and its 9 categories, limiting diversity and risking correlation with LlavaGuard's distribution. The five policy levels are hand-designed by the authors, with L1/L5 deliberately 'counterintuitive'; absolute F1 stays low even for the best model (Ours(RL+SafeEditTrain) overall 49.43 on SafeEditBench; L2 only 23.08), so the benchmark is far from solved and cross-policy generalization is improved, not achieved. Data pipeline depends on closed/proprietary components (Gemini 'Nano Banana' editor, permissive Gemma-27B recaptioner), and the ~100K SFT images are 'internet-sourced' with unstated provenance. The verifiable reward is only the binary label, so reasoning quality is supervised only indirectly. Reported baseline numbers for other methods are cited rather than re-run. No adversarial/robustness evaluation against deliberate attacks despite motivating evasion.

datasets_used: SafeEditBench (this paper's own; 128 edited images across 9 LlavaGuard categories, 62 image pairs labeled under 5 policies L1-L5, built from LlavaGuard test set via Nano Banana / Gemini-2.5-Flash-Image edits)
UnsafeBench (9 harmful categories; evaluation benchmark, prompted with OpenAI content policy)
~100K internet-sourced unsafe images for Stage-1 SFT recaptioning
LlavaGuard training set (reused for Stage-2 policy-conditioned RL; also basis for SafeEditTrain)
General VQA: MMMU, RealWorldQA, BLINK, MMT-Bench
quantitative_results: UnsafeBench overall (Table 2): Ours(Full) 72.2, Ours(SFT) 67.0, GLM-4V-9B 56.5, InstructBLIP 55.9, ShieldGemma2 47.3, QwenGuard-7B 43.6, Qwen2.5-7B 41.7, Llama Guard 22.7
Table 4 QwenGuard-7B over-specialization: LlavaGuard 84.57 but UnsafeBench 43.56, SafeEditBench 32.76, General Overall 35.98 (BLINK 12.05)
Table 4 Ours(RL): LlavaGuard 71.78, UnsafeBench 62.39, SafeEditBench 45.59, General Overall 57.02; vs Qwen2.5-7B General Overall 59.55
Table 3 policy overfit: SFT-on-L1 = 100/0/0/0/0 across L1-L5; RL-on-L4 = 43.22/19.18/38.64/75.2/73.61
Table 5 ablation (UnsafeBench / General): Qwen2.5-VL-7B 41.71/56.92; w/o Recap SFT 53.22/54.51; Ours(SFT) 66.96/53.37; Ours(Full) 72.16/53.09 (RL adds +5.2)

Table 6 SafeEditBench F1 overall: Ours(RL+SafeEditTrain) 49.43 (best), Qwen2.5-VL-7B 48.68, Ours(RL) 45.59, LlavaGuard-7B 44.16, GLM-4V-9B 41.16, QwenGuard-7B 32.76, LlavaGuard-0.5B 30.52, ShieldGemma2 27.50, Llama Guard 22.73, QwenGuard-3B 5.19
baselines_compared: Traditional classifiers: NudeNet, NSFW Detector, MultiHeaded, SD Filter
General-purpose VLMs: Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Qwen2-VL-7B, LLaVA-V1.6-7B, InstructBLIP, GLM-4V-9B
Safety guardrails: Llama Guard, QwenGuard-7B, QwenGuard-3B, ShieldGemma2, LlavaGuard-7B, LlavaGuard-0.5B
